prompt: After a blameless postmortem for an ops agent that took a wrong action in production, what is the AI-specific follow-up that makes the agent fleet safer over time?
choices: Delete the agent's decision trace so the mistake isn't retained
Turn the misfire into a new guardrail/policy and an offline eval case the agent's policy must pass, so the same wrong action can't silently recur
Switch off the action audit trail to reduce noise
Give the agent broader permissions so it can fix its own mistakes next time
explanation: A unit test won't catch a non-deterministic wrong action, so the operational loop is to convert every misfire into a guardrail/policy and an eval case. That's how the fleet's policy grows teeth and the same wrong action can't quietly return.

prompt: Why add per-agent cost anomaly detection and action-rate caps when agents act in production? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Each agent step is an LLM call and may spin up resources, so a looping agent burns money and compute fast
Anomaly detection alerts on deviation from the trend, catching a runaway early — not just at a fixed threshold
It removes the need to attribute cost or actions to any specific agent
An action-rate cap bounds the damage of a loop and can force the agent to stop and escalate
explanation: Agent actions compound cost and risk, so anomaly detection gives early warning of a runaway and the action-rate cap bounds it and forces escalation. Neither removes the need for per-agent attribution — that's exactly how you find which agent to stop.

prompt: Which are sound ways to operate an agent fleet safely at scale and under attack? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A kill-switch that pauses all agent autonomy instantly, plus revert of the action
Least-privilege identities and approval gates for high-blast actions, applied uniformly via policy-as-code
Treat logs, tickets, and error messages the agent reads as untrusted input that can't authorise high-blast actions
Give every agent standing unlimited production write access so incidents resolve faster
explanation: A kill-switch, least-privilege policy-as-code with approval gates, and treating external text as untrusted input all bound what a misfiring or compromised agent can do. Standing unlimited prod write is the opposite of least privilege and turns one bad action — or one injection — into a fleet-wide catastrophe.

prompt: Why version an agent's full policy (prompt + tools + permissions + model) and roll changes out via shadow then canary, instead of editing it live?
choices: Because versioning makes the agent run faster

Because the agent acts on the world, so a change can silently alter what actions it takes; shadow compares proposed actions on real events before any execute, canary limits blast radius, and a policy flag gives a one-flip rollback
Because a versioned policy never needs to be updated again
Because live editing is required to enable autonomous actions
explanation: Since the agent takes real actions, a policy or model change can quietly make it act differently. Treating the policy as a versioned artifact and rolling out by shadow (dry-run, compare to humans) → canary (small autonomous slice) → flagged rollout lets you catch wrong actions before prod and revert in one flip.
